subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_text: Monochromatic light of frequency 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz is produced by a laser. The power emitted is 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 W. What is the energy of a photon in the light beam? How many photons per second, on an average, are emitted by the source?
question_html:
Monochromatic light of frequency 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 Hz is produced by a laser. The power emitted is 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 W.

  1. What is the energy of a photon in the light beam?
  2. How many photons per second, on an average, are emitted by the source?
solution_text: Each photon has an energy {tex}E=h v=\left(6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{J} \mathrm{s}\right)\left(6.0 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{Hz}\right){/tex} , here h being Planck's constant {tex}=3.98 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}{/tex} If N is the number of photons emitted by the source per second, the power P transmitted in the beam equals N times the energy per photon E, so that P = NE. Then {tex}N=\frac{P}{E}=\frac{2.0 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{W}}{3.98 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}}{/tex} {tex}=5.0 \times 10^{15}{/tex} photons per second.
solution_html:
1. Each photon has an energy
    {tex}E=h v=\left(6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{J} \mathrm{s}\right)\left(6.0 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{Hz}\right){/tex}, here h being Planck's constant 
    {tex}=3.98 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}{/tex}
  2. If N is the number of photons emitted by the source per second, the power P transmitted in the beam equals N times the energy per photon E, so that P = NE. Then
    {tex}N=\frac{P}{E}=\frac{2.0 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{W}}{3.98 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}}{/tex}
    {tex}=5.0 \times 10^{15}{/tex} photons per second.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_text: The work function of caesium is 2.14 eV. Find the threshold frequency for caesium, and the wavelength of the incident light if the photocurrent is brought to zero by a stopping potential of 0.60 V.
question_html:
The work function of caesium is 2.14 eV. Find

  1. the threshold frequency for caesium, and
  2. the wavelength of the incident light if the photocurrent is brought to zero by a stopping potential of 0.60 V.
solution_html:
1. For the cut-off or threshold frequency, the energy h{tex}\nu_0{/tex} of the incident radiation must be equal to a work function {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex}. So that
    {tex}\nu_{0}=\frac{\phi_{0}}{h}=\frac{2.14 \mathrm{eV}}{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{2.14 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}}{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{J} \mathrm{s}}=5.16 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{Hz}{/tex}
    Thus, for frequencies less than this threshold frequency, no photoelectrons are ejected.
  2. Photocurrent reduces to zero when the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons equals the potential energy eV0 by the retarding potential V0. Einstein's Photoelectric equation is 
    {tex}e V_{0}=h \nu-\phi_{0}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}-\phi_{0}{/tex}
    or, {tex}\lambda=h c /\left(e V_{0}+\phi_{0}\right){/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\left(6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}\right)}{(0.60 \times1.6\times10^{-19}+2.14 \times1.6\times10^{-19})}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{19.89 \times 10^{-26} \mathrm{Jm}}{(2.74 \mathrm{eV})}{/tex}
    Thus the wavelength is given by:-
    {tex}\lambda=\frac{19.89 \times 10^{-26} \mathrm{Jm}}{2.74 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}}=454 \mathrm{nm}{/tex}

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_text: What is the de Broglie wavelength associated with an electron moving with a speed of 5.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 m/s, and a ball of mass 150 g traveling at 30.0 m/s?
question_html:
What is the de Broglie wavelength associated with

  1. an electron moving with a speed of 5.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 m/s, and
  2. a ball of mass 150 g traveling at 30.0 m/s?
solution_text: For the electron; Mass m {tex}=9.11 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{kg}{/tex} , speed {tex}v=5.4 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex} p = mv = 9.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -31 (kg) {tex}\times{/tex} 5.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 (m/s) p = 4.92 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -24 kg m/s de Broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda = \frac {h}{p}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}}{4.92 \times 10^{-24} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0.135 nm For the ball; Mass m' = 0.150 kg, speed v' = 30.0 m/s Then momentum p' = m'v' = 0.150 (kg) {tex}\times{/tex} 30.0 (m/s) p' = 4.50 kg m/s de Broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda ' = \frac {h}{p'}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{- 34} \mathrm{Js}}{4.50 \times \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda^{\prime}=1.47 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. For the electron;
    Mass m {tex}=9.11 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{kg}{/tex}, speed {tex}v=5.4 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex}
    p = mv = 9.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-31 (kg) {tex}\times{/tex} 5.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 (m/s)
    p = 4.92 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-24 kg m/s
    de Broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda = \frac {h}{p}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}}{4.92 \times 10^{-24} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}}{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0.135 nm
  2. For the ball;
    Mass m' = 0.150 kg, speed v' = 30.0 m/s
    Then momentum p' = m'v' = 0.150 (kg) {tex}\times{/tex} 30.0 (m/s)
    p' = 4.50 kg m/s
    de Broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda ' = \frac {h}{p'}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{- 34} \mathrm{Js}}{4.50 \times \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}}{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda^{\prime}=1.47 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{m}{/tex}

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_text: Find the maximum frequency, and minimum wavelength of X-rays produced by 30 kV electrons.
question_html:
Find the

  1. maximum frequency, and
  2. minimum wavelength of X-rays produced by 30 kV electrons.
solution_html:
Here, V = 30kV {tex} = 30 \times {10^3}V = 3 \times {10^4}V{/tex}

  1. h{tex}\nu{/tex}max = eV , Hence maximum frequency is ,
    {tex}{\nu_{\max }} = \frac{{eV}}{h} = \frac{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}} \times 3 \times {{10}^4}}}{{6.63 \times {{10}^{ - 34}}}}{/tex}
    {tex} = 7.24 \times {10^{18}}Hz{/tex}
  2. {tex}{\lambda _{\min }} = \frac{c}{{{\nu_{\max }}}} = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{7.24 \times {{10}^{18}}}}m{/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow {\lambda _{\min }} = 0.414 \times {10^{ - 10}} m = 0.414\mathop A\limits^o {/tex}

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_text: The work function of caesium metal is 2.14 eV. When light of frequency {tex}6 \times {10^{14}}Hz{/tex} is incident on the metal surface, photoemission of electrons occurs. What is the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electrons, stopping potential, and maximum speed of the emitted photoelectrons?
question_html:
The work function of caesium metal is 2.14 eV. When light of frequency {tex}6 \times {10^{14}}Hz{/tex} is incident on the metal surface, photoemission of electrons occurs. What is the

  1. maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electrons,
  2. stopping potential, and
  3. maximum speed of the emitted photoelectrons?
solution_html:
1. {tex}K.E{._{\max }} = h\nu - {\phi _0} = \frac{{6.63 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 6 \times {{10}^{14}}}}{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}} - 2.14{/tex}
    {tex}{K.E_{\max }} = 0.35\;eV{/tex}
  2. {tex}e{V_0} = K.E{._{\max }}{/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow {V_0} = \frac{{K.E{._{\max }}}}{e} = \frac{{0.35\;eV}}{e} = 0.35V{/tex}
  3. {tex}\frac{1}{2}mv_{\max }^2 = 0.35\;eV = 0.35 \times 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}}J{/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow {v_{\max }} = \sqrt {\frac{{0.35 \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}} \times 2}}{{9.1 \times {{10}^{ - 31}}}}} {/tex}
    {tex} = 3.34 \times {10^5}m{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_text: The photoelectric cut-off voltage in a certain experiment is 1.5 V. What is the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons emitted?
question_html: The photoelectric cut-off voltage in a certain experiment is 1.5 V. What is the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons emitted?
solution_text: Photoelectric cut-off voltage, v 0 = 1.5 v The maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons is given as; K e = eV 0 Where, e = Charge on an electron = 1.6 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 -19 C {tex}\therefore K_{e}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.5{/tex} {tex}=2.4 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}{/tex} Therefore, the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons emitted in the given experiment is 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J
solution_html:
Photoelectric cut-off voltage, v0 = 1.5 v
The maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons is given as;
Ke = eV0
Where,
e = Charge on an electron = 1.6 {tex}\times {/tex} 10-19 C
{tex}\therefore K_{e}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.5{/tex}
{tex}=2.4 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}{/tex}
Therefore, the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons emitted in the given experiment is 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_text: Monochromatic light of wavelength 632.8 nm is produced by a helium neon laser. The power emitted is 9.42 mW. Find the energy and momentum of each photon in the light beam. How many photons per second, on the average, arrive at a target irradiated by this beam? (Assume the beam to have uniform cross-section which is less than the target area), and How fast does a hydrogen atom have to travel in order to have the same momentum as that of the photon?
question_html:
Monochromatic light of wavelength 632.8 nm is produced by a helium neon laser. The power emitted is 9.42 mW.

  1. Find the energy and momentum of each photon in the light beam.
  2. How many photons per second, on the average, arrive at a target irradiated by this beam? (Assume the beam to have uniform cross-section which is less than the target area), and
  3. How fast does a hydrogen atom have to travel in order to have the same momentum as that of the photon?
solution_text: Given, Wavelength, {tex}\lambda = 632.8nm = 632.8 \times {10^{ - 9}}m{/tex} Frequency, {tex}\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda } = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{632.8 \times {{10}^{ - 9}}}}{/tex} {tex} = 4.74 \times {10^{14}}Hz{/tex} E = h {tex}\nu{/tex} {tex} = 6.63 \times {10^{ - 34}} \times 4.74 \times {10^{14}}{/tex} {tex} = 3.14 \times {10^{ - 19}}J{/tex} Also, p (momentum) {tex} = \frac{h}{\lambda } = \frac{{6.63 \times {{10}^{ - 34}}}}{{632.8 \times {{10}^{ - 9}}}} = 1.05 \times {10^{ - 27}}kg\;m{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} Power emitted, P = 9.42 mW {tex} = 9.42 \times {10^{ - 3}}W{/tex} P = nE {tex}n = \frac{P}{E} = \frac{{9.42 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{3.14 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}} = 3 \times {10^{16}}photons/\sec {/tex} Velocity of hydrogen atom {tex} = \frac{{Momentum\;'p'\;of\;{H_2}\;atom\;}}{{Mass\;of\;{H_2}\;atom\;}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow v = \frac{{1.05 \times {{10}^{ - 27}}}}{{1.673 \times {{10}^{ - 27}}}}{/tex} = 0.63 ms -1
solution_html:
Given,
Wavelength, {tex}\lambda = 632.8nm = 632.8 \times {10^{ - 9}}m{/tex}
Frequency, {tex}\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda } = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{632.8 \times {{10}^{ - 9}}}}{/tex} {tex} = 4.74 \times {10^{14}}Hz{/tex}

  1. E = h{tex}\nu{/tex}
    {tex} = 6.63 \times {10^{ - 34}} \times 4.74 \times {10^{14}}{/tex}
    {tex} = 3.14 \times {10^{ - 19}}J{/tex}
    Also, p (momentum) {tex} = \frac{h}{\lambda } = \frac{{6.63 \times {{10}^{ - 34}}}}{{632.8 \times {{10}^{ - 9}}}} = 1.05 \times {10^{ - 27}}kg\;m{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
  2. Power emitted, P = 9.42 mW {tex} = 9.42 \times {10^{ - 3}}W{/tex}
    P = nE
    {tex}n = \frac{P}{E} = \frac{{9.42 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{3.14 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}} = 3 \times {10^{16}}photons/\sec {/tex}
  3. Velocity of hydrogen atom
    {tex} = \frac{{Momentum\;'p'\;of\;{H_2}\;atom\;}}{{Mass\;of\;{H_2}\;atom\;}}{/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow v = \frac{{1.05 \times {{10}^{ - 27}}}}{{1.673 \times {{10}^{ - 27}}}}{/tex} = 0.63 ms-1

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_text: In an experiment on photoelectric effect, the slope of the cut-off voltage versus frequency of incident light is found to be {tex}4.12 \times {10^{ - 15}}Vs{/tex} . Calculate the value of Planck's constant.
question_html: In an experiment on photoelectric effect, the slope of the cut-off voltage versus frequency of incident light is found to be {tex}4.12 \times {10^{ - 15}}Vs{/tex}. Calculate the value of Planck's constant.
solution_html:
The slope of the cut-off voltage versus frequency of incident light
{tex}\frac V\nu = 4.12 \times {10^{ - 15}}Vs {/tex}
V is related to frequency by the equation,
h{tex}\nu{/tex} = eV
So,
{tex}h = \frac{eV}\nu{/tex}
{tex}h = 4.12 \times {10^{ - 15}} \times 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow h = 6.592 \times {10^{ - 34}}Js{/tex}

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_text: The threshold frequency for a certain metal is {tex}3.3 \times {10^{14}}Hz{/tex} . If light of frequency {tex}8.2 \times {10^{ 14}}Hz{/tex} is incident on the metal, predict the cut-off voltage for the photoelectric emission.
question_html: The threshold frequency for a certain metal is {tex}3.3 \times {10^{14}}Hz{/tex}. If light of frequency {tex}8.2 \times {10^{ 14}}Hz{/tex} is incident on the metal, predict the cut-off voltage for the photoelectric emission.
solution_html:
Here it is given that, 
{tex}{\nu _0} = 3.3 \times {10^{14}}Hz{/tex}, {tex}\nu = 8.2 \times {10^{14}}Hz{/tex}, {tex}h = 6.63 \times {10^{ - 34}}Js{/tex}
Using Einsteins photoelectric equation,
{tex}\frac{1}{2}mv_{\max }^2 = h(\nu - {\nu _0}) = e{V_0}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {V_0} = \frac{{h(\nu - {\nu _0})}}{e}{/tex}
{tex}{V_0} = \frac{{6.62 \times {{10}^{ - 34}}}}{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}\left( {8.2 \times {{10}^{14}} - 3.3 \times {{10}^{14}}} \right){/tex}
V0 = 2.03 V

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_text: The work function for a certain metal is 4.2 eV. Will this metal give photoelectric emission for incident radiation of wavelength 330 nm?
question_html: The work function for a certain metal is 4.2 eV. Will this metal give photoelectric emission for incident radiation of wavelength 330 nm?
solution_text: {tex}{\phi _0} = 4.2eV = 4.2 \times 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}}J = 6.72 \times {10^{ - 19}}J{/tex} {tex}E = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow E = \frac{{6.62 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{330 \times {{10}^{ - 9}}}} = 6.018 \times {10^{ - 19}}J{/tex} As energy of incident photon {tex}E < {\phi _0}{/tex} , hence no photoelectric emission will take place.
solution_html:
{tex}{\phi _0} = 4.2eV = 4.2 \times 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}}J = 6.72 \times {10^{ - 19}}J{/tex}
{tex}E = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow E = \frac{{6.62 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{330 \times {{10}^{ - 9}}}} = 6.018 \times {10^{ - 19}}J{/tex}
As energy of incident photon {tex}E < {\phi _0}{/tex}, hence no photoelectric emission will take place.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_text: Light of frequency {tex}7.21 \times {10^{14}}Hz{/tex} is incident on a metal surface. Electrons with a maximum speed of {tex}6.0 \times {10^5}m/s{/tex} are ejected from the surface. What is the threshold frequency for photoemission of electrons?
question_html: Light of frequency {tex}7.21 \times {10^{14}}Hz{/tex} is incident on a metal surface. Electrons with a maximum speed of {tex}6.0 \times {10^5}m/s{/tex} are ejected from the surface. What is the threshold frequency for photoemission of electrons?
solution_html:
Here, {tex}\nu = 7.21 \times {10^{14}}Hz{/tex}
{tex}{v_{\max }} = 6.0 \times {10^5}m{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex}m = 9 \times {10^{ - 31}}kg{/tex}
Applying Einsteins photoelectric equation,
{tex}K{E_{\max }} = \frac{1}{2}mv_{_{\max }}^2 = h(\nu - {\nu_0}){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}mv_{\max }^2 = h(\nu - {\nu_0}){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {\nu_0} = \nu - \frac{{mv_{\max }^2}}{{2h}}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {\nu_0} = 7.21 \times {10^{14}}{/tex}{tex} - \frac{{\left( {9.1 \times {{10}^{ - 31}}} \right) \times {{\left( {6 \times {{10}^5}} \right)}^2}}}{{2 \times \left( {6.63 \times {{10}^{ - 34}}} \right)}}{/tex}
{tex} = 4.74 \times {10^{14}}Hz{/tex}

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_text: Light of wavelength 488 nm is produced by an argon laser which is used in the photoelectric effect. When light from this spectral line is incident on the emitter, the stopping (cutoff) potential of photoelectrons is 0.38 V. Find the work function of the material from which the emitter is made.
question_html: Light of wavelength 488 nm is produced by an argon laser which is used in the photoelectric effect. When light from this spectral line is incident on the emitter, the stopping (cutoff) potential of photoelectrons is 0.38 V. Find the work function of the material from which the emitter is made.
solution_text: Given, Wavelength {tex}\lambda = 488nm = 488 \times {10^{ - 9}}m{/tex} Stopping potential, V 0 = 0.38 V As, {tex}e{V_0} = h\nu - {\phi _0}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e{V_0} = h\frac{c}{\lambda } - {\phi _0}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {\phi _0} = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda } - e{V_0}{/tex} {tex} = \left( {\frac{{6.63 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{488 \times {{10}^{ - 9}} \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}} \right.{/tex} {tex}\left. { - \frac{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}} \times 0.38}}{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}} \right){/tex} = (2.55 - 0.38)eV = 2.17 eV
solution_html:
Given,
Wavelength {tex}\lambda = 488nm = 488 \times {10^{ - 9}}m{/tex}
Stopping potential, V0 = 0.38 V
As, {tex}e{V_0} = h\nu - {\phi _0}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow e{V_0} = h\frac{c}{\lambda } - {\phi _0}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {\phi _0} = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda } - e{V_0}{/tex}
{tex} = \left( {\frac{{6.63 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{488 \times {{10}^{ - 9}} \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}} \right.{/tex} {tex}\left. { - \frac{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}} \times 0.38}}{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}} \right){/tex}
= (2.55 - 0.38)eV
= 2.17 eV

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_text: What is the de Broglie wavelength of a bullet of mass 0.040 kg travelling at the speed of 1.0 km/s, a ball of mass 0.060 kg moving at a speed of 1.0 m/s, and a dust particle of mass 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 kg drifting with a speed of 2.2 m/s?
question_html:
What is the de Broglie wavelength of

  1. a bullet of mass 0.040 kg travelling at the speed of 1.0 km/s,
  2. a ball of mass 0.060 kg moving at a speed of 1.0 m/s, and
  3. a dust particle of mass 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 kg drifting with a speed of 2.2 m/s?
solution_text: Mass of the bullet, m = 0.040 kg Velocity with which the bullet is travelling, v = 1 kms -1 = 10 3 ms -1 using the formula of momentum, p = mv {tex}=0.040 \times 10^{3}{/tex} = 40 kg ms -1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} De-broglie wavelength of bullet is given by :- {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34}}{40}{/tex} {tex}=1.7 \times 10^{-35} \mathrm{m}{/tex} Mass of the ball, m = 0.060 kg Velocity with which the balls is moving, v = 1.0 ms -1 Momentum of the particle, p = mv = 0.060 kg ms -1 Therefore, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex} De-broglie wavelength of the ball is given by {tex}=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34}}{0.060}{/tex} {tex}=1.1 \times 10^{-32} m{/tex} Mass of the dust particle, m {tex}=1.0 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{kg}{/tex} Velocity of the particle, v = 2.2 m/s Momentum of the particle, p = mv = 2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 kg ms -1 Therefore, De-broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34}}{2.2 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} {tex}=3 \times 10^{-25} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Mass of the bullet, m = 0.040 kg
    Velocity with which the bullet is travelling, v = 1 kms-1 = 103 ms-1
    using the formula of momentum,
    p = mv
    {tex}=0.040 \times 10^{3}{/tex}
    = 40 kg ms-1
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} De-broglie wavelength of bullet is given by :-
    {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34}}{40}{/tex}
    {tex}=1.7 \times 10^{-35} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
  2. Mass of the ball, m = 0.060 kg
    Velocity with which the balls is moving, v = 1.0 ms-1
    Momentum of the particle, p = mv = 0.060 kg ms -1
    Therefore,
    {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex}
    De-broglie wavelength of the ball is given by {tex}=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34}}{0.060}{/tex}
    {tex}=1.1 \times 10^{-32} m{/tex}
  3. Mass of the dust particle, m {tex}=1.0 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{kg}{/tex}
    Velocity of the particle, v = 2.2 m/s
    Momentum of the particle, p = mv = 2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 kg ms-1
    Therefore,
    De-broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34}}{2.2 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex}
    {tex}=3 \times 10^{-25} \mathrm{m}{/tex}

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_text: Show that the wavelength of electromagnetic radiation equal to the de Broglie wavelength of its quantum (photon).
question_html: Show that the wavelength of electromagnetic radiation equal to the de Broglie wavelength of its quantum (photon).
solution_text:
de-Broglie wavelength of a photon, {tex}\lambda =  rac{h}{p}{/tex} Momentum of a photon, {tex}p =  rac{{h
u}}{c}{/tex} Hence, {tex}\lambda =  rac{h}{{ rac{{h
u}}{c}}} =  rac{c}{
u}{/tex} It is same as wavelength of electromagnetic radiation.
solution_html:
de-Broglie wavelength of a photon,
{tex}\lambda = \frac{h}{p}{/tex}
Momentum of a photon, {tex}p = \frac{{h\nu}}{c}{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\lambda = \frac{h}{{\frac{{h\nu}}{c}}} = \frac{c}{\nu}{/tex}
It is same as wavelength of electromagnetic radiation.
